Yet even in these circumstances, there is real danger, real courage, and an intimation of transcendence. July 20: Introduced by VIFF Year-Round Programmer, Tom Charity

film_slugs.title: Robby Müller's America
film_slugs.synopsis: Nobody has more evocatively documented the red deserts, fluorescent green dive bars, and gray urban landscapes of America than Dutch cinematographer Robby Müller. In this presentation, cinematographer and professor Devan Scott will delve into the ways in which Müller, in collaboration with directors such as Wim Wenders (Paris, Texas), William Friedkin (To Live and Die in L.A.), and Jim Jarmusch (Down by Law), spent the 1980s rendering visual poetry out of the dark, forgotten corners of the American experiment. Media Partner

film_slugs.title: Paris, Texas
film_slugs.synopsis: A lonely man strides through Monument Valley in self-imposed exile for sins he can no longer remember. Nursed back to health by his brother, Travis attempts to restore the broken lives he has on his conscience. Think about the story too closely and the film’s threads start to unravel, yet the film’s patient attention to behaviour combined with cinematographer Robby Muller’s enraptured response to the American landscape makes for a deeply felt authenticity. Cult supporting player Harry Dean Stanton is unforgettable in his only lead role. Produced in America with European financing, Paris, Texas crystallized Wenders’ love affair with the West and the wide open spaces that inspire his deepest reveries. It won the Palme d’Or and was an art house hit across Europe. From a script by Sam Shepard and LM Kit Carson (whose son Hunter plays Travis’s kid) with an indelible score by Ry Cooder.

film_slugs.title: Repo Man
film_slugs.synopsis: Englishman Alex Cox swapped Oxford for UCLA and pulled together this sun-baked punk black comedy, his debut feature and a very cool cult item. Delinquent dropout Otto (Emilio Estevez) gets mixed up in the car repossession trade when he’s conned into helping old pro Bud (character actor Harry Dean Stanton, making the most of killer lines like, “Look at those assholes. Ordinary fucking people. I hate ’em”). Bud is only the first of what turns into a memorable parade of cynical crackpots, including Tracy Walter’s street philosopher and Fox Harris, terrific as a lobotomized scientist driving the radioactive 64 Chevy Malibu everybody wants to get their hands on (just don’t look in the trunk). It’s one of the earliest movies to get a bead on how the US of A was tripping towards Crazyville. Cox would go on to make several more interesting movies, good and bad, including Sid and Nancy, Walker and Straight to Hell, but this is his funniest and most entertaining effort; it has a kind of Roger Corman, drive-in movie feel, with an anarchic edge. The soundtrack includes Iggy Pop, Blag Flag, and The Plugz, and there’s a brief appearance from The Circle Jerks. Robby Muller – who would also work with Harry Dean Stanton on Paris, Texas at this time, was the DP, and finds a kind of desolate beauty in the concrete wastelands of So Cal.

film_slugs.title: Blood Simple
film_slugs.synopsis: You can’t keep a dead man down. Not in the Coens’ bravura suspense thriller. The brothers’ first movie is a delectably twisted film noir set in modern day (well, mid 80s) Texas, where seedy bar-owner Marty (Dan Hedaya) doesn’t much care for the fact that his wife Abby (Frances McDormand) is playing around behind his back. The Coens are riffing on the work of pulp novelist James L Cain here (Double Indemnity; The Postman Always Rings Twice), and at the very least they deliver brilliant pastiche. Someone called it the “most promising American directorial debut since Citizen Kane” – a rush of hyperbole that at least conveys the exhilaration of coming face to face with prodigious talent. Blood Simple bursts with malicious wit and audacious style (cinematographer Barry Sonnenfeld isn’t one to keep his camera in the background). It was Frances McDormand’s first movie, and her college roommate Holly Hunter can be heard as a voice on the phone at one point. But the unforgettable performance comes from veteran character actor M Emmett Walsh as a sleazy private dick with a very personal, very Texan, notion of ethics. July 22: Introduced by Karen Lam

film_slugs.title: Desperately Seeking Susan
film_slugs.synopsis: Mired in suburban New Jersey wedlock Roberta (Rosanna Arquette) turns to the personal ads for vicarious romance, with unexpected results. She’s obsessed with the recurring ad which gives the movie its name, and one day her curiosity gets the better of her. But a case of mistaken identity and a bump on the head turns her hitherto boring life upside down. Susan Seidelman brings a hip ’80s SoHo sensibility to this emancipated screwball comedy. Madonna has never found a better fit than this, her first movie role, the thrift-store free spirit Susan — and even then Rosanna Arquette gives as good as she gets with a deliciously kooky comic turn as the freshly liberated housewife. Devotees of American indie cinema should keep an eye out for John Turturro, Richard Edson, John Lurie, Laurie Metcalf, Will Patton, Giancarlo Esposito, Anne Carlisle, Victor Argo, Richard Hell and Rockets Redglare, among others, a who’s who of the new wave scene-makers.

film_slugs.title: Suburbia
film_slugs.synopsis: Penelope Spheeris can take credit for three singularly bleak, angry films that came out of her interest in the American punk music scene in the late 1970s/early 1980s: the felicitously-named documentary The Decline of Western Civilization (1980); the Roger Corman produced punk drama Suburbia (1984); and the killing spree exploitatation flick The Boys Next Door (1985). Not coincidentally, these were her first three films. Greek-American by birth (Costa-Gavras is a cousin), Spheeris grew up traveling with her father’s carnival, the Magic Empire. She studied film at UCLA and produced skits for Albert Brooks on the first season of the long-running comedy TV show Saturday Night Live. Intrigued by LA’s punk rock scene, Spheeris wrote, directed and edited The Decline of Western Civilisation, a valuable record of bands like The Circle Jerks, The Germs, Fear and Phranc. She transitioned to narrative features with this Roger Corman-produced portrait of a gang of punks who call themselves T.R. – The Rejected. Living in an abandoned suburban development alongside a metaphorical pack of wild dogs, The Rejected carry the scars of abuse, intolerance, and violence, and there’s no question that the writer-director is on their side of the tracks. Look out for Flea, from the Red Hot Chili Peppers, in a supporting role. With music from The Vandals.

film_slugs.title: Masculinity in 80s Cinema
film_slugs.synopsis: “On your feet, soldier!” Machismo came roaring back in the 80s: Sylvester Stallone (Rambo), Arnold Schwarzenegger (The Terminator), Mel Gibson (Mad Max), Harrison Ford (Indiana Jones), Eddie Murphy (Beverly Hills Cop) and a young man by the name of Tom Cruise (Top Gun) were the biggest box office stars in the world. UBC Film Prof Ernest Mathijs examines what it meant to be a real, dutiful man in American movies in the Reagan era, and to check manhood in the mirror. This mid-length lecture (25 mins) will include film clips, some of which may be violent, and potentially also some polysyllabic words, though these will be used sparingly. Media Partner

film_slugs.title: First Blood
film_slugs.synopsis: In his 1970s breakthrough roles, Sylvester Stallone typically played a working class underdog. In the Reagan era he transformed himself into a more conventional action hero — still often an outsider, maligned or misunderstood, but practically invulnerable. The underdog became an ubermensch, a super man. That transition is played out before our eyes in First Blood (1983), in which “Sly” plays the loner Vietnam War veteran John Rambo. In this first film, adapted from David Morrell’s novel, he’s first and foremost a victim, traumatized by the war and let down by the authorities on his return from that defeat. He’s also, in Canadian Ted Kotcheff’s film, a danger to himself and to everyone around him. Yet by the time the inevitable sequels rolled in, Rambo was recast as a formidable one-man army, a Cold War action icon who might single-handedly turn the tide in Afghanistan. Audiences lapped it up: Stallone became the most highly paid star in Hollywood. At $12 million a picture he was earning twice his nearest rival, and John Rambo was everywhere. Even Ronald Reagan took to invoking him as a role model. Set in Washington but filmed in Hope, BC, First Blood is a gripping survivalist action movie, much better than what was to come. Media Partner

film_slugs.title: The Terminator
film_slugs.synopsis: Maybe Arnie would have made it anyway – he never doubted it for a second – but it’s no secret the moment he became a bone fide movie star. It was the scene in the original Terminator when he visualizes several possible responses to an irritating cleaner, and chooses the most vulgar. Somehow the line was even funnier in Arnie’s thick Austrian accent. When he followed this by wiping out an entire police station audiences went crazy for him, to the point where writer-director James Cameron had to rewrite the Terminator as a good guy in the sequel. In this first film, he’s bad news. A killer cyborg sent back to 1984 from 2029 to erase humanity’s last hope of combatting the rise of the machines before he’s even been born. The movie is lean, relentless, intense, as Elvis Mitchell put it in the New York Times, “a B-movie with flair.”

film_slugs.title: Ghostbusters
film_slugs.synopsis: Altogether now: “If there’s something strange/In your neighborhood/Who you gonna call?” That’s right. Ectoplasm Eliminators R Us. Ousted from their cushy university positions, a team of paranormal researchers decide to go private and offer their services to the public. Luckily for them, New York City in those dark days was bursting with ghosts, poltergeists, and sundry supernatural spectres in all shapes and sizes. Bill Murray’s smirky, insouciant Dr Peter Venkman cemented his image in the popular imagination: a slacker ironist always above the fray, part Groucho Marx, part Elliott Gould. While Harold Ramis’s nerdy Egon Spengler functions as a fine straight man, he and co-writer Dan Aykroyd (Raymond Stantz) channeled all the funniest lines into their costar’s mouth. Ernie Hudson barely gets a look in as the the diversity squad rep. Directed by Ivan Reitman, Ghostbusters runs amok with its Reaganite ideological underpinnings (private enterprise good; environmental regulation bad) but the wisecracks keep on coming, the goofy ghouls and cool gadgets are great fun, and this movie is always a blast with a good crowd.

film_slugs.title: A Nightmare on Elm Street
film_slugs.synopsis: “One, two, Freddy’s coming for you…” Springwood teenagers are having trouble not sleeping – they’re knocking back coffee, popping pills, anything to delay another date with their dream stalker (which may explain why these high school students look like college grads). When one of them does drop off, the bed sheets turn red. Wes Craven’s 1984 slumber party massacre started from scratch – it was a low budget item from what was then a genuine independent studio, New Line – and became the iconic horror franchise of the decade. With his battered fedora, striped sweater, a razor-glove and a face like Sal’s Famous, Freddy Krueger was the most personable boogie man Hollywood had come up with in a long time. So what if he was also a child abuser? A recurring nightmare who stalked teenagers as they slept, Freddy preyed on the defenseless and played fast and loose with physics: if Salvador Dali had created a slasher movie villain, he might have come up with someone like Freddy. And yes, that is Johnny Depp in his feature film debut sporting a crop top and jogging pants. July 28: Introduction by Rachel Talalay (filmmaker)

film_slugs.title: Lost in America
film_slugs.synopsis: Albert Brooks doesn’t get the credit he deserves: Lost in America has a solid claim to be the finest American comedy of the 1980s. An advertising man, David is incensed when he’s overlooked for a promotion. With his wife Linda’s blessing, he quits his job, buys a Winnebago, and they set out to find the real America, like Peter Fonda and Dennis Hopper in his favourite movie, Easy Rider. But their hippie odyssey gets off to a rocky start in Vegas — where they plan to renew their wedding vows — when Linda (Julie Haggerty) loses the “nest egg” (all their savings) in a night of madness at the roulette tables. As the Rolling Stones put it: “You can’t always get what you want…”, or perhaps Joni Mitchell said it better: “Don’t it always seem to go, that you don’t know what you’ve got, till it’s gone.” July 29: Introduction by novelist Sam Wiebe (Sunset & Jericho)

film_slugs.title: Witness
film_slugs.synopsis: When an Amish child witnesses a murder, big city police detective John Book (Harrison Ford) has to go undercover in the luddite community to ensure the boy’s protection. Although the script could easily have furnished a generic fish-out-water cop thriller, Australian director Peter Weir (Master and Commander; The Truman Show) responds with such fascination to the Amish society the film becomes something more captivating and mysterious. A wordless sequence in which the community comes together to build a wooden barn lingers long in the memory. This thriller turns out to be at least as much a romance. Kelly McGillis shines bright as the boy’s (widowed) mother, and she brings out a side of Harrison Ford we haven’t seen too often. Weir’s sympathetic, largely uncritical take on the Amish community is a long way from how Sarah Polley portrayed the Mennonites in Women Talking, but the movie is intimate and soulful enough to suck us in. Media Partner

film_slugs.title: Mala Noche
film_slugs.synopsis: Sounding the first note of what would come to be known as New Queer Cinema, Gus Van Sant’s low budget indie debut was shot on the streets of Portland, Oregon, on 16mm, and it is based on Walt Curtis’s autobiographical novel. It’s so real you half expect to walk out into Portland when it’s over. Walt (Tim Streeter) is a lonely convenience store clerk who has fallen in love with a Mexican migrant worker named Johnny (Doug Cooeyate). Though Walt has little in common with the ambivalent object of his affections — including a shared language — his desire to possess Johnny prompts a sexual awakening that results in taboo trysts and a tangled love triangle.

film_slugs.title: Desert Hearts
film_slugs.synopsis: “Donna Deitch’s swooning and sensual first narrative feature, Desert Hearts, was groundbreaking upon its release in 1985: a love story about two women, made entirely independently, on a shoestring budget, by a woman. In this 1959-set film, adapted from a beloved novel by Jane Rule, straitlaced East Coast professor Vivian Bell (Helen Shaver) arrives in Reno to file for divorce but winds up catching the eye of someone new, the free-spirited young Cay (Patricia Charbonneau), touching off a slow seduction that unfolds against a breathtaking desert landscape. With undeniable chemistry between its two leads, an evocative jukebox soundtrack, and vivid cinematography by Robert Elswit (There Will Be Blood), Desert Hearts beautifully exudes a sense of tender yearning and emotional candor.” (Janus Films) Media Partner

film_slugs.title: Streets of Fire
film_slugs.synopsis: In Walter Hill’s neon-dipped rock n roll fable, Diane Lane is Ellen Aim, lead singer of Ellen Aim and the Attackers, is kidnapped – snatched from the stage – by a biker gang, The Bombers, led by Raven Shaddock (Willem Dafoe). Ellen’s ex beau, Tom Cody (Michael Pare), agrees to lead a rescue op. Hill — hot off 48 Hrs — set out to make a comic book movie, except he didn’t like comic books, so he and screenwriter Larry Gross created a world of their own, strip-mined from Hill’s memories of what he loved as a teenager: “Custom cars, kissing in the rain, neon, trains in the night, high-speed pursuit, rumbles, rock stars, motorcycles, jokes in tough situations, leather jackets and questions of honor.” He went on: “By design, it’s comic strip in orientation, mock-epic in structure, movie-heroic in acting style, operatic in visual style, and cowboy-cliche in dialogue.” There were other elements in the mix too: the success of Flashdance, the emergence of MTV, the ongoing Star Wars phenomenon… The concept was to create a stylized but serious drama for teenagers, a musical fantasy but with muscle, and no one over the age of 30… Does it work? The movie was a costly flop at the box office, but has since become a cult favourite. Jim Steinman’s songs Tonight Is What It Means to be Young and Nowhere Fast, along with Ry Cooder’s soundtrack, seal the deal. Veteran British critic Philip French reckoned it was “pure cinema”. In the New York Times Janet Maslin found it “misogynistic”, which is hard to square with Amy Madigan’s standout supporting turn, but you can see what she meant. For sure it looks dazzling on the big screen, a film out of time, a shimmering dream of a movie.

film_slugs.title: To Live and Die in LA
film_slugs.synopsis: French Connection /The Exorcist director William Friedkin reputedly lost his mojo in the late 70s… but this 1985 thriller is right up there with his best work and it’s plugged right into the synthetic heart of the new decade. William L Petersen — successfully auditioning for Michael Miami Vice Mann’s Manhunter — is reckless FBI agent Dick Chance, and bent on avenging his late partner, a victim of ruthless counterfeit artist Eric Masters (Willem Dafoe, who was cornering the market in heavies back then). When his first plan to entrap Masters results in another law enforcement death, Chance’s new partner (John Pankow) starts to get cold feet. With its Wang Chung soundtrack, hard-edged Robby Muller cinematography, and deeply cynical screenplay, this is Reagan-noir, as cold as it is bright. Friedkin even tops his famous French Connection chase scene with an eight-minute pursuit down a six lane highway going against the traffic flow — this has to be seen to believed. Robert Yeoman – future Wes Anderson DP – photographed the sequence when Muller balked at the risk. August 2: Introduced by Marc Staehling

film_slugs.title: Post-Modernity in Lumberton
film_slugs.synopsis: After the commercial misfire of Dune, David Lynch went to the suburbs and pulled off the most hotly debated and influential American movie of the decade. Blue Velvet mixed the familiar with the deeply strange, it broke taboos in the depiction of sex and violence (and repetitive swearing), and in its post-modern mix-and-match of cinematic styles: the coming-of-age film, noir mystery, camp comedy, melodrama, and surrealism. A film that both lingers and boils over. In this mid-length film talk, UBC professor Ernest Mathijs explores an endlessly fascinating film in the context of Lynch’s artistic development, the nostalgic impulse in 80s culture, and dark sexuality in Hollywood movies. Media Partner

film_slugs.title: Something Wild
film_slugs.synopsis: A graduate of the Roger Corman school of drive-in exploitation pictures, director Jonathan Demme hit a home run with this quirky road comedy about a straightlaced businessman (Jeff Daniels) kidnapped for the weekend by sexy good time gal “Lulu” (Melanie Griffith). Of course, it turns out she has ulterior motives, and Lulu is not her real name. But she’s not the only one with secrets. Even boring Charlie has a wild side (“I am a closet rebel,” he admits. “I just channeled my rebellion into the mainstream.”) The movie zigs and zags and sometimes it zags when you expect it to zig. (The screenplay was the first from E Max Frye). The tone is light and larky, there are cool cameos by other indie filmmakers (John Waters; John Sayles) and the soundtrack is electic and poppy and also cool, until a big switch in the last act when Ray Liotta enters the scene — this was his first movie role and he’s electrifying, terrifying, almost like he broke in from a different film altogether. It’s a 90s genre mashup five years ahead of the curve. But by then Jonathan Demme would be making The Silence of the Lambs.

film_slugs.title: Blue Velvet (4K Restoration)
film_slugs.synopsis: It’s impossible to disagree with Brett Easton Ellis’s assessment that this is (with Something Wild) the key film of the 1980s. It was a like a revolutionary action, like a bomb going off in the cosy surrounds of studio cinema. Like so many other movies of the decade, Blue Velvet appeared to hark back to an earlier, more innocent era… a sleepy, safe midwestern town with white picket fences where everyone knows everyone else… even after he decides to play amateur detective, young Jeffrey Beaumont (Kyle MacLachlan) evokes the Hardy Boys he’s so straight. But what he finds after he happens across a severed ear in a vacant lot, that’s another, altogether more disturbing story. Violent sex, drugs, corruption, this is big city toxicity and Jeffrey — a curious voyeur, just like us — is sucked right into the vortex. A subversive pop artifact and an Oedipal nightmare, the film blends and bends between a knowing post-modern ambiance that’s often camp, and shocking eruptions of taboo-busting sex, violence and narcotic delirium. Lynch broke the unwritten rules of cinematic decorum, and many were outraged by his trespasses. Yet this provocative, brilliant, funny, erotic and upsetting movie continues to fascinate and inspire. It acknowledges shadows we’d rather keep to ourselves, but you don’t have to look very far or very hard to see aspects of the ugly truths Jeffrey discovers all around us.

film_slugs.title: Brazil
film_slugs.synopsis: Sam Lowry (Jonathan Pryce) is a clerk intelligent enough to work the system, but who generally keeps his head down – until he catches a glimpse of his dream girl (Kim Greist). That’s when everythings starts to go wrong… Inspired by George Orwell’s totalitarian allegory 1984 (and made in that very year), but inflected with Kafka (and cowritten with playwright Tom Stoppard) the film paints Big Brother as bureaucracy-run-amok, snuffing out even the merest spark of individuality. Any resemblances to Hollywood were immediately brought home: produced for $15 million, the film was distributed by Twentieth Century Fox in a 143 minute cut everywhere except North America, where Universal held the rights – and where studio boss Sidney Scheinberg prepared his own 94 minute version, complete with a happy ending. With the film-makers and the studio at loggerheads, Gilliam eventually went public, taking out a full page advertisement in Variety reading “Dear Sid Scheinberg, when are you going to release my movie Brazil? Terry Gilliam.” The effrontery worked – aided by many critics who included the unreleased film among their best of the year lists. Although the compromise 132 minute US version failed to take off at the box office Brazil’s reputation and cult following has continued to grow. This is Gilliam’s richest and most coherent work, an astonishing amalgam of surrealism, black comedy, Orwell, Kafka and Thatcher, and what the director dubbed “retro-futurist” design. It’s one of the visionary films of the 80s. Media Partner

film_slugs.title: Aliens
film_slugs.synopsis: If Alien was a slasher movie in space, then James Cameron’s sequel ups the ante by incorporating war movie tropes. Instead of one extra-terrestrial killer on the loose, now we’ve got an entire colony facing off against a squad of hard-bitten marines. (It’s the same scenario as in Avatar, except that Cameron has reversed his sympathies.) The movie’s suspense and action build inexorably, but there’s time for Sigourney Weaver’s Ripley to establish meaningful, authentic relations with one of the marines, Hicks (Michael Biehn, from Terminator) and with Newt, the orphaned girl who is the sole human survivor on an outpost overrun by xenomorphs. Weaver was nominated for the Academy Award for Best Actress for her work here, rare recognition for a woman in a genre film but entirely justified in this case. There’s a real case to be made that Ripley is the first female action hero. And while you could argue about the “masculine” qualities that the (male) filmmakers value in her, there’s no question that in her strength, resilience, and her intelligence (often disregarded by the men in these movies, to their peril) Ripley has been a legitimate inspiration to many women, and changed the way that women have portrayed on screen.

film_slugs.title: Near Dark
film_slugs.synopsis: Vampires came back in fashion as the 80s progressed, often as a metaphor for the AIDS epidemic. But more than anyone, it was Kathryn Bigelow (The Hurt Locker) who rebooted the image of the bloodsuckers. In place of the aristocratic European count in his cape, Bigelow gives us trailer trash, an itinerant band of down and dirty murderous misfits (including Lance Henriksen, Jenette Goldstein and an immense Bill Paxton from Aliens), lost rebels who haunt the Oklahoma highways in an RV with blacked out windows looking for rednecks to feed on. New (reluctant) convert Caleb has ethical reservations when it comes to murder, and that too is a new wrinkle in the texture of the tale. The group dynamics suggest an elective family, or perhaps a pack of wolves, but Bigelow paints these outsiders without sentimentality, as the parasites they are. With a score by Tangerine Dream.

film_slugs.title: Platoon
film_slugs.synopsis: The first Vietnam War movie made by a Vietnam War veteran, Oliver Stone’s Platoon was a more authentic “boots on the ground” movie than either Apocalypse Now or The Deer Hunter. Stone — like his protagonist, Chris (Charlie Sheen) had volunteered to serve in 1967. He wrote the screenplay in 1975 but it took ten years before he was allowed to make it. In the film, Chris is a true believer, but his faith in his country’s righteousness is sorely tested. Stone furnishes him with two opposing father figures: Sgts Elias (Willem Dafoe, finally getting to play a good guy) and Barnes (Tom Berenger), hellbent on killing. Platoon doesn’t give us a Vietnamese perspective, but it doesn’t flinch from conveying the brutalizing effects of war and the crimes committed in its name.

film_slugs.title: Dirty Dancing
film_slugs.synopsis: 1963. Baby is seventeen and itching to enjoy her youth before it’s too late. At first blush a sleepy summer resort in the Catskills with her parents and her sister does not seem promising. But then she catches sight of dance instructor Johnny Castle (Patrick Swayze) and changes her mind. He’s a working class kid but handsome and charming and a terrific dancer, and she’s quick on her feet too. You could guess the rest, but would you guess that abortion is an important plot point? Eleanor Bergstein’s screenplay is smart and sympathetic, and Kenny Ortega delivers sizzling choreography.

film_slugs.title: Ferris Bueller's Day Off
film_slugs.synopsis: “Life moves pretty fast. If you don’t stop and look around once in a while, you could miss it.” For a ten-year spell, roughly between 1984 and 1994, John Hughes produced hit after hit after hit. In an astonishing burst of creativity he wrote 20 produced screenplays in that period, and directed eight of them, starting with Sixteen Candles (1984) and including The Breakfast Club (1984), Weird Science (1985), Ferris Bueller’s Day Off (1986), and Planes, Trains and Automobiles (1987). For Gen X-ers, Hughes was what Marvel is to today’s kids, and maybe more, because he made films about them. He introduced several new stars in the process, Molly Ringwald, Emilio Estevez, Ally Sheedy, Anthony Michael Hall, John Cusack – the core of the so-called Brat Pack – plus Macaulay Culkin. Most importantly, he reinvented the teen movie in his own image. Hughes was born in 1950, in Michigan – a privileged, comfortable time and place to come into the world. His father worked in sales, the family was firmly middle class, and if there was much wrong with this world it never showed up in his films (“I didn’t have a tortured childhood,” he told the New York Times in 1991. “I liked it.”). When Hughes was 13 they moved to Chicago, the setting for many of his movies and, along with Matthew Broderick, the star attraction in Ferris Bueller’s Day Off. Hughes made teen comedies but mostly they weren’t raunchy or rebellious. He treated teens like grownups, with respect, and his movies are mostly chaste, a little romantic, and small c conservative. But with Ferris Bueller he cut loose a little bit. Ferris (Broderick) is a law unto himself, a benevolent con artist, a prodigy when it comes to skipping school. “I’ve wanted to be Ferris my whole life,” the director Jason Reitman admitted once. Frankly, we all do. He’s Tom Sawyer in shades, driving a Ferrari, living the life. The August 13, 11:30 am screening of Ferris Bueller’s Day Off is a relaxed screening. Relaxed screenings are open to ANYONE who would benefit from a less restrictive and sensory-friendly experience including (but not limited to) people living with dementia; Autistic people; people with learning difficulties; people with a sensory or communication disorder; parents with young babies; and anyone who feels they would benefit from a more supportive and inclusive experience. How much are tickets? That’s up to you! Pay what you can for tickets to relaxed screenings. We do not want the cost of tickets to hinder your ability to attend. Click on “Book Tickets” and select the amount ($0, $7, $15, $20) you can pay per ticket. At a relaxed screening you can expect: Relaxed Screenings Presented by Back to the 80s Media Partner

film_slugs.title: Yuppies!
film_slugs.synopsis: Yuppies were a new breed in the 1980s: Young Urban Professionals. They were ambitious, successful, cynical, always rushed… and plagued by nightmares, if the movies had it right. Films like After Hours, Something Wild, Fatal Attraction, Wall Street and Working Girl had fun at the expense of these upwardly mobile sharks, with varying degrees of envy, admiration and contempt. Ernest Mathijs opens their rolodex, clocks their Rolex, and runs their credit scores. Media Partner

film_slugs.title: Fatal Attraction
film_slugs.synopsis: “The zeitgeist movie of the decade,” according to Time magazine, Fatal Attraction is the story of a one night stand that gets out of control. Dan (Michael Douglas) is a happily married lawyer who nevertheless cheats on his wife (Anne Archer) with Alex (Glenn Close), a book editor he meets through work. For Dan it’s just casual sex. But when he makes his exit, something in Alex snaps. She wants more from him. And the more dismissive he is, the angrier she gets. Adrian Lyne’s high concept thriller is one of the more notorious examples of a film being rewritten after test screenings. While the filmmakers had sought to balance sympathies between Dan and Alex, the audience bayed for blood, and a controversial but cathartic new climax gave them exactly what they wanted.Even so, the film was seen as a Rorschach test highlighting the tensions between the sexes, and it was a huge box office hit. “Years hence, it will be possible to pinpoint the exact moment that produced ’’Fatal Attraction,’’ Adrian Lyne’s new romantic thriller, and the precise circumstances that made it a hit. It arrived at the tail end of the having-it-all age, just before the impact of AIDS on movie morality was really felt. At the same time, it was a powerful cautionary tale. And it played skillfully upon a growing societal emphasis on marriage and family, shrewdly offering something for everyone: the desperation of an unmarried career woman, the recklessness of a supposedly satisfied husband, the worries of a betrayed wife. What’s more, it was made with the slick, seductive professionalism that was a hallmark of the day.” Janet Maslin, New York Times

film_slugs.title: Down By Law
film_slugs.synopsis: Jim Jarmusch defined what would become a fertile east coast hipster indie scene, even though he did his best to get out of New York. Compensating for low budgets with charm and charisma, he concocted a series of minimalist slow burn comedies, scribbling on the margins with cool musos like Lounge Lizard John Lurie and troubadour Tom Waits. Here, they’re two surly Americans forced to share a small jail cell. A third cellmate, an Italian tourist who barely speaks English, transforms the mood with his sunny disposition and eccentric communication style, especially after he hatches a surprisingly effective escape plan… Top DP Robby Muller (Paris, Texas; To Live and Die in LA) does the honours again, this time in black and white. Jim Jarmusch is back at VIFF this year with another triptych of short vignettes (also featuring Tom Waits): Father, Mother, Sister, Brother.

film_slugs.title: Who Framed Roger Rabbit? and the American Animation Renaissance
film_slugs.synopsis: After decades of postwar decline, the early 1980s seemed to spell the end of feature-length animation in the United States, and Walt Disney Studios considered shuttering its animation division. Yet by the end of the decade, Who Framed Roger Rabbit? was released to immense box office success and wide critical acclaim, marking the beginning of an American animation renaissance. Why did a studio synonymous with wholesome family entertainment take a risk on a technically daring film crammed with slapstick comedy and sexual innuendo? Following the film’s detective protagonist, Dr. Gadassik (Emily Carr University) investigates the mystery of how Hollywood animation went from the brink of collapse to one of the world’s top theatrical attractions. Her talk uncovers shifting alliances and rivalries, the return of adult-oriented animation after decades of repression, and the rise of digital effects that would forever blur the line between animation and live-action. Toontown will never be the same. Media Partner

film_slugs.title: Hollywood Shuffle
film_slugs.synopsis: Hollywood didn’t afford opportunities to minorities in the 1980s. BIPOC filmmakers rarely broke through. BIPOC actors were relegated to stereotypical supporting roles (if they were lucky). Robert Townsend and cowriter Keenan Ivory Wayans lay it all out in this pointed satire about an aspiring Black actor doing the rounds, auditioning for slaves, butlers, and hustlers, dreaming of something bigger and better. Making a virtue of necessity, this shoestring production is basically a series of comic skits, lampooning lazy filmmaking and unthinking white racism with cheerful abandon. The “Black Acting School” is a particular highlight.

film_slugs.title: Robocop - Restored Director's Cut
film_slugs.synopsis: Detroit, sometime soon. Amid mounting violent crime, the Omni corporation has been entrusted with the police force, and quickly introduces its secret weapon: Robocop. Part man, part machine, Robocop is an invulnerable and deadly crime-fighting tool imprinted with three foolproof directives: to uphold the law, serve the public trust, and protect the innocent. What could go wrong? Gory, fast-paced, and very funny, Robocop is also a fine piece of classical storytelling. Ostensibly a sci-fi thriller, but at least as much a political satire on the smiling face of Reaganomics Paul Verhoeven’s first American-made film is as subversive as the best graphic novels, and no less relevent today than it was in ’87. August 18: Introduced by Jason Deline

film_slugs.title: Bull Durham
film_slugs.synopsis: A former minor league ball player himself, writer-turned-director Ron Shelton hit a home run with this somewhat autobiographical romantic comedy, the best baseball movie — maybe the best sports movie — ever made. The joy of Bull Durham is that it doesn’t matter if you care about sport going in. The movie makes you care because the characters are interesting and they love the game: veteran Crash (Kevin Costner), a minor league legend who brings gravitas and experience to the scattershot Bulls; hot, erratic pitcher Nuke LaLoosh (Tim Robbins), the very definition of a loose cannon; and above all Annie Savoy (Susan Sarandon), the team’s number one fan and groupie, but also a cultured, funny life coach in her own right. “Perhaps Bull Durham has resonated all these years because it’s about loving something more than it loves you back. It’s about reckoning. it’s about loss. It’s about men at work trying to survive in the remote outposts of their chosen profession. It’s about the women they fall for, and who fall for them. It cannot be dismissed that it’s also about the joy of playing a game for a living. It’s about team and connections and risk and reward […] It’s romantic, and it’s supposed to be funny, and despite what most fans of the movie say, it is also about baseball.” Ron Shelton, The Church of Baseball

film_slugs.title: Midnight Run
film_slugs.synopsis: Proving once again it’s not what you do but the way that you do it, this straightforward road comedy is a classic of its kind by virtue of its odd couple casting of Robert De Niro as jaded bounty hunter (Jack Walsh) and Charles Grodin as a white collar mob accountant (nicknamed “The Duke”) who embezzled millions and has accumulated a long list of enemies. Jack is to escort the Duke from New York to Los Angeles, but when the latter refuses to fly what should have been an easy job — a midnight run — turns into a hilariously dangerous odyssey. Beverly Hills Cop director Martin Brest handles the action scenes impressively enough but it’s the acting which takes this to another level. Handcuffed together for most of the running time, De Niro and Grodin are the sour cream and onion of comedy duos, an unexpectedly perfect combo. August 19: Introduced by Marc Staehling

film_slugs.title: Who Framed Roger Rabbit
film_slugs.synopsis: Filmmakers have mixed animation and live action at least since Tom and Jerry danced with Gene Kelly, but the integration reached a whole new level with Who Framed Roger Rabbit, which imagines “Toontown” as a suburb of Los Angeles, close to Hollywood, but a parallel reality which exists by its own laws of loony physics. Set in 1947, the film is a noir private eye mystery. Bob Hoskins is wonderful as the shamus, Eddie Valiant, hired to find out if drop-dead gorgeous Jessica Rabbit is having an extra-marital affair. The plot is satisfying, but it rightly takes a back seat to the comedy. Eddie’s the straight man to the hilarious slapstick antics of Roger (the animation was directed by Richard Williams, who was recognized with a special Academy Award). Aug 20: Introduced by Michael van den Bos, Animation History Teacher, Capilano University

film_slugs.title: sex, lies and videotape
film_slugs.synopsis: It’s appropriate that our Back to the 80s series should climax with Steven Soderbergh’s first feature and with Spike Lee’s Do the Right Thing, both released in 1989, and both showcased at the Cannes Film Festival — where Soderbergh’s movie would win the Palme d’Or, much to Lee’s disgust. The 80s was the decade of home video. Camcorders were introduced in 1983. And a camcorder plays a key role in sex, lies… Ann (Andie MacDowell) is oblivious to the affair going on between her husband John (Peter Gallagher) and her sister Cynthia (Laura San Giacomo). But when John’s college friend Graham (James Spader) comes, his peculiar hobby — taping women talking about their sexual experience — will change everything (just as video technology would eventually upend the film industry). The film is an astringent character comedy, very well acted (San Giacomo is the standout, though her career never went anywhere). It was the first breakout hit for Harvey Weinstein’s Miramax company, who picked it up after it won the Audience Award at Sundance, paving the way for the crossover success of the independent movie sector in the 1990s. August 25: Introduced by VIFF Feature Film Coordinator, John Hollands

film_slugs.title: They Live
film_slugs.synopsis: “I have come here to chew bubblegum and kick ass… and I’m all out of bubblegum.” We began our Back to the 80s series with Back to the Future and John Carpenter’s Escape from New York. As we wrap up the season it’s time to take another look at the work of Carpenter, and his most explicitly political movie, the cult favourite They Live — also, categorically the greatest film to star a professional wrestler. When a drifter, Nada (“Rowdy” Roddy Piper), discovers a very special pair of sunglasses, it gives him a whole new perspective on life. See, these are a kind of x-ray specs. They reveal the true nature of the world we live in: the power elite is in fact an alien race, feeding off our apathy through the promotion of rampant consumerism. Far-fetched… ?

film_slugs.title: Matewan
film_slugs.synopsis: “The Prince of Darkness is upon the land,” rails a preacher near the beginning of John Sayles’s historical drama. “Now in the Bible his name is Beezlebub, Lord of the Flies. Right now on Earth today, his name is Bolshevist! Socialist! Communist! Union man! Lord of untruth, sower of evil seed, enemy of all that is good and pure. And this creature walks among us…. What are we going to do about it?” The preacher is played by Sayles himself, and it’s fair to say that he sounds an awful lot like too many of today’s right-wing politicians. Matewan is based on a real historical atrocity, a violent confrontation between striking miners and mercenary strike breakers that took place in West Virginia, 1920. One of the pioneer American independents, and one of the few who define “independent” in a political sense, John Sayles had considerable success ploughing his own furrow in the 1980s, writing, directing and producing films like Return of the Secaucus Seven, Lianna, Baby It’s You, Brother From Another Planet and Eight Men Out. A novelist before he became a filmmaker via the Roger Corman school of exploitation quickies (He scripted Piranha and Alligator), Sayles’ intelligence is obvious, and his dedication to filming low-key blue collar stories no one else would touch is admirable. Matewan, which draws on the iconography and conventions of the western (not typically a vehicle for progressive narratives)… as Michael Wilmington wrote in the LA Times, it’s “more an American folk epic than social realism, an anti-western.” In this, it’s a companion piece of sorts to Michael Cimino’s Heaven’s Gate.

film_slugs.title: Do the Right Thing
film_slugs.synopsis: Spike Lee burst onto the film scene with She’s Gotta Have It in 1986. It was a low budget breakout, sexy, funny, fresh. But his second film, properly funded this time, was a massive step forward, not only for Lee but for American Cinema. It’s the hottest day of a long, hot summer, and tensions in Bed-Stuy, New York are ready to boil. But for Mookie (played by Lee himself) it’s just another day spent crisscrossing the neighbourhood, delivering pizza for Sal (Danny Aiello). From the bristling title sequence (Public Enemy’s Fight the Power, over Rosie Perez’s punchy, sexy dance moves) through to its combustible climax, the movie is incredibly dynamic and exuberant, its saturated colours and barbed comic exchanges creating a heightened realism that’s appropriate for the pent up racial tensions the film taps into while its portrait of police brutality and endemic racism remains all too relevant. Don’t take our word for it, this is what Barack Obama had to say about it: “Do the Right Thing still holds up a mirror to our society, and it makes us laugh and think, and challenges all of us to see ourselves in one another.”

film_slugs.title: The Princess Bride
film_slugs.synopsis: “My name is Inigo Montoya! Prepare to die!” One of the most beloved movies of the 80s, Princess Bride delivered a classic swashbuckling fairy tale for kids who thought they knew better. Beautiful Buttercup (Robin Wright) is kidnapped and held for the odious Prince Humperdinck (Chris Sarandon). Her sweetheart Westley (Cary Elwes), now returned as the Dread Pirate Roberts, attempts to save her. On the way he meets an accomplished swordsman (Mandy Patinkin) and a kindly giant (pro wrestler Andre the Giant), who become his companions on the quest. The framing device – Peter Falk reading the tale to his grandson (Fred Savage) – is an inspired touch, a wink towards cynicism, and the copious comedy incorporates Monty Pythonesque grotesquerie and mischief (it sets the stage for Shrek, for sure) but in truth this is genuinely romantic throwback, every bit as thrilling as Errol Flynn’s adventures in the 1930s or Douglas Fairbanks’ in the 1920s.

film_slugs.title: Hairspray
film_slugs.synopsis: Once upon a time little John Waters was condemned to the cult midnight movie circuit for cheapo bad taste extravaganzas like Multiple Maniacs, Pink Flamingos, and Desperate Living. Gradually, though, something shifted. It wasn’t so much that Waters sold out, more that the mainstream came to meet him halfway, which is more or less what transpired when he wrote and directed the original version of Hairspray in 1988. A nostalgic, tongue-in-cheek tribute to the subversive art of shaking your tail-feather in Waters’ native Baltimore, circa 1962, Hairspray is the story of footloose working class teen Tracy Turnblad (Ricki Lake). Tracy lives for The Corny Collins Show, a live cable TV show, which showcases the latest rhythm and blues moves with its troupe of Baltimore’s nicest kids. One day she gets her chance to audition for the show, but a big girl like Tracy doesn’t fit the profile any more than the “negroes” who it is feared may corrupt the white children. Rallying the kids, Tracy does her bit to redefine the face of pop culture… (Turns out, there really was a Baltimore music show which was canceled over just this issue.) Waters’ movie is not strictly a musical (in as much as all the singing and dancing is naturalistic), but it feels like one. The story’s triumph-of-the-underdog structure is the stuff of 42nd Street legend, and scarcely ten minutes go by without another tune from Gene Pitney, The Five Du-Tones, or Jerry Dallman and the Knightcaps.

film_slugs.title: Synth Scores
film_slugs.synopsis: Giant sweeping layers of sine wave melodies in Vangelis’ Blade Runner, performed on the Yamaha CS-80. Metallic crushing bliss in Brad Fiedel’s The Terminator, performed on the Prophet 5. The vibrant twisting emotions and dark urgent textures in Tangerine Dream’s Near Dark, combining synthetic drums with orchestration. The 80’s provided the film world with an instrument that would change soundtracks forever: the synthesizer. But what is a synthesizer? What makes a synthesizer score different? Join composer Cayne McKenzie in discussion: how synthesizers shaped the soundtracks of the 80s and beyond. Cayne McKenzie composed soundtracks for Violent and Ash, and with the bands We Are the City and Big Kill Media Partner

film_slugs.title: Mystery Film
film_slugs.synopsis: Tonight’s movie will be a surprise. We’re not revealing the title, the filmmaker, the stars… All you know is that it’s a quintessential American movie from the latter half of the 1980s. It’s not a blockbuster but it feels to us like a suitable climax to this year’s summer season. Media Partner

film_slugs.title: The Thing
film_slugs.synopsis: We asked, you answered: The Thing was the runaway winner of our fan choice contest, handily besting Amadeus and leaving Batman trailing in its wake. Filmed in Stewart, British Columbia, John Carpenter’s film is the second adaptation of John W Campbell’s novella ’Who Goes There?”, previously filmed by Howard Hawks and Christian Newby in 1951 as The Thing From Another World. Set in American Antarctic research station, the film follows the discovery of a similar Norwegian site, the scene of some recent unexplained horrors that claimed the lives of everyone on the base. Records show that the Norwegians recently came across a UFO buried in the ice… Legendary for the shocking creature fx created by Rob Bottin, the 1982 Thing is now widely perceived as a classic in its own right, one of the scariest films ever made.

film_slugs.title: Movie Poster Design
film_slugs.synopsis: The 1980s were – arguably – the last great era of movie poster design. Key movie art, so central to the shaping of audience expectations for decades, was about to undergo a massive shift with the release of the ground-breaking photo-editing software, Photoshop, in February 1990. Photoshop led to the increasing use of close-up photos of a film’s star(s) and a precipitous decline in poster artistry throughout the 90s and beyond. Take this visual journey through 1980s poster design and the works of some of the great poster artists of the era like Drew Struzan, Bob Peak and John Alvin, among others. As the ’80s were also an era of excess and bad taste – the ramifications of which are still being felt today (witness current US politics) – no journey through 1980s movie posters would be complete without taking a side-trip through some of the worst examples of 80s poster excesses. Guest speaker Kris Elgstrand was born in Vancouver in the 70s but grew up in Coquitlam from the 70s to the 90s. A filmmaker and performer, several of Kris’ shorts and features have screened at VIFF and elsewhere over the years. As part of his mid-life crisis, he has recently returned to one of his early passions: collecting and appreciating movie posters. Media Partner

film_slugs.title: Mash Potato Time
film_slugs.synopsis: In this two part Film Studies talk, director/producer Rachel Talalay discusses the impact of video cassettes on movie making in the 1980s, and her partnership with John Waters. Part 1: Bring back Blockbuster! How Videocassettes made the 80s Initially feared as the death knell for moviegoing, the videocassette market turned out to be a bonanza for Hollywood studios. It transformed movie-making in the 1980s and made genre king… Starting out in the independent sector at that time, first as an accountant for New Line Studios, then reinventing herself as a producer on the Nightmare on Elm Street series (and then a director in her own right), Rachel Talalay talks about her first-hand experience of the 80s film-making trenches. Part 2: It’s Mash Potato Time: Hairspray John Waters calls Hairspray a “Trojan Horse of a movie”. Why has this pro-integration extravaganza starring the legendary drag queen Divine survived American anti-woke culture wars? And why did no one at the time think the movie had legs? But don’t get too serious, this is a John Waters movie. Come put some gravy on your mashed potatoes, dance the dirty boogie — and tease up that hair, hon. Media Partner

series.description: Enjoy great movies at the luxurious VIFF Centre, voted one of the best cinemas in Vancouver and home to the prestigious Vancouver International Film Festival.

series.name: Best of 2022

film_slugs.title: Triangle of Sadness
film_slugs.synopsis: Social satirist Ruben Östlund (The Square; Force Majeure) picked up his second Palme d’Or at Cannes earlier this year with this gleefully mischievous assault on the State of Things. Influencer couple Carl and Yaya (Harris Dickinson and Charlbi Dean) join a luxury cruise for the super rich. While the crew bends over backwards to accommodate the passengers’ whims (even one woman’s insistence that the entire staff stops what they are doing to enjoy a swim), the captain refuses to leave his cabin before the mandatory dinner given in his name—a tumultuous banquet which coincides with a dire turn in the weather. Östlund retains his knack for hitting on potent and provocative situations—the banquet is an unforgettable, uproarious set piece presided over by a gloriously unflappable, stiffly inebriated Woody Harrelson. Östlund really turns the screws in the film’s second half, when a shipwreck upends the social hierarchy and suddenly, all bets are off. Granted, most of the characters are truly awful and the satire is subtle as a baseball bat. Yet the movie keeps building and damn if we don’t care about them by the end. It’s a must-see.

film_slugs.title: Hit the Road
film_slugs.synopsis: In his feature debut, Panah Panahi turns the road movie on its head, adding suspense and ambiguity to the gags and character studies that the genre has made familiar. Hit the Road is the story of one family and their mysterious journey through rural Iran. From early on, it’s made clear that the clan is on some sort of mission, and the drama slowly escalates as details are revealed. Along the way, there are laughs, moments of reverie, and plenty of opportunities for the cast to shine—six year-old Rayan Sarlak gives an especially winning performance as the bratty but endearing runt of the pack. The son of Jafar Panahi (No Bears), Panah shares his father’s bent for political subversion, and he’s similarly inclined toward limpid, sharply composed images, but there is an energy and exuberance here which is his own.

film_slugs.title: Aftersun
film_slugs.synopsis: The strong resemblance and stronger rapport between Calum (Paul Mescal), 30, and Sophie (Frankie Corio), his 11-year-old daughter, has them frequently mistaken for siblings. Away on a family-of-two vacation at a discount Turkish resort, the pair often conduct themselves like impish kids playing hooky. Prone to manic spells, Calum showers Sophie with affection and whatever else his meagre budget might allow. All of this in a desperate bid to stave off a depressive episode that looms like the Sword of Damocles. In her astonishing feature debut, Charlotte Wells demonstrates a gentle touch but unwavering command of emotional complexities. As Sophie fumbles her way into adolescence and Calum teeters at the brink of psychological collapse, telling moments and revelatory gestures amass significance. Wells’ formal prowess is impressive as she interweaves her luminous 35mm compositions with digital video keepsakes shot by Sophie. As Aftersun builds to its deeply affecting finale, we’re reminded how life’s turning points often arrive without warning.

film_slugs.title: Memoria
film_slugs.language: In Spanish and English with English subtitles
film_slugs.synopsis: The latest from Apichatpong Weerasethakul is a dreamy, hallucinatory meditation on sound, memory, and history starring Tilda Swinton and set in beautiful Colombia. Swinton plays Jessica, an expat orchid grower with a strange malady: she recurrently experiences a booming sound that no other character can hear. The quest for answers takes Jessica first to a recording studio, then to the Amazon jungle; in each location, she finds men who offer assistance, but no clear solutions. Weerasethakul may be one of the greatest filmmakers of our time, but he’s not an artist for whom clarity is a priority: his interest lies in the poetic and the mysterious, and on that score, Memoria is a resounding success. It’s also a stunningly beautiful work, with images that will linger in the mind along with the teasing enigmas, the strange sounds, and the feeling of having emerged from a splendid dream. “Astonishing… It left a residue of happiness in my heart.” Peter Bradshaw, The Guardian

film_slugs.title: Slash/Back
film_slugs.language: In English and Inuktitut with English subtitles
film_slugs.synopsis: Seemingly nestled safely away in the Earth’s northern reaches, the sleepy hamlet of Pangnirtung, Nunavut transforms into a chaotic battleground as Inuit teenagers go toe-to-bodysnatched-toe with alien invaders who’ve slithered into the skins of local policemen and wildlife. While the odds are stacked against Maika (Tasiana Shirley), Uki (Nalajoss Ellsworth), and Jesse (Alexis Vincent-Wolfe), they know their horror movie tropes, descend from a long line of hunters, and aren’t about to take any nonsense for extraterrestrial invaders. Drawing from both Hollywood classics like The Thing and traditional Inuit stories of the shapeshifting Ijiraq, Nyla Innuksuk’s exuberant debut harnesses genre cinema’s allegorical power to revisit colonial histories and explore contemporary Indigeneity, while also delivering enough suspense and spectacle to put many studio efforts to shame. Bolstered by its gregarious performances and suffused with a sense of community (it employed a largely local crew), Slash/Back possesses a warrior spirit that’s as endearing as it is indomitable. 94% Fresh, Rotten Tomatoes Nyla Innuksuk Originally from Igloolik, Nunavut, Nyla Innuksuk studied film at Ryerson University before working as a producer at the Indigenous-owned Big Soul Productions. She explores emerging technologies and interactive works through her company Mixtape VR and is a writer for Marvel Comics, where she co-created the teenage superhero Snowguard. + Short film: Nalujuk Night (Jennie Williams, Canada, 2021) 13 mins From the dark of the Nunatsiavut night, the Nalujuit appear on the sea ice. They walk on two legs, yet their faces are animalistic, skeletal, and otherworldly… A special New Year’s treat from Inuk filmmaker Jennie Williams.

film_slugs.title: RRR (Rise Roar Revolt)
film_slugs.synopsis: The most fun we had at the movies in 2022, the astonishing RRR is the kind of blockbuster action film that makes a mockery of cultural borders and reminds us how and why cinema has thrilled audiences all over the world for more than a century. India, 1920. The British Raj still holds sway. Two future real-life Indian revolutionaries, Komaram Bheem (Jr. NTR) and Alluri Sitarama Raju (Ram Charan) collide in an exhilaratingly cockamamie plot involving a girl kidnapped by an evil English aristocrat. The pair may be at cross purposes but it’s impossible not to root for them both in an escalating series of death-defying action adventure set pieces. “RRR Is the Best — and Most Revolutionary — Blockbuster of 2022… if any movie ever deserved to be seen on a giant screen with a crowd, it’s this one. RRR is about the movies: the thrill of watching stories told at larger-than-life levels, the joy of watching stars collide , the effort of rendering lavish mythologies at whatever the digital equivalent of 24 frames per second is, the sensation of seeing manufactured movement via gleefully conspicuous special effects bump up against genuine physical effort.” David Fear, Rolling Stone

film_slugs.title: Marcel the Shell With Shoes On
film_slugs.synopsis: The most charming documentary of the year turns out to be a portrait of a rare mollusk adapting to environmental change, a comedy about separation and loneliness, a touching drama about caring and friendship, and many other things beside (most obviously, to be sure, an animated entertainment for all the family). First time feature filmmaker Dean Fleisher-Camp plays filmmaker Dean Fleisher-Camp, recently divorced, now renting, and for want of anything better to do training his camera on the eponymous Marcel, a plucky and intelligent snail who speaks with the voice and the wit of Fleisher-Camp’s cowriter, Jenny Slate. Separated from all of his community bar his grandmother, Nana Connie (voiced by entomophile Isabella Rossellini), Marcel makes a worthy interview subject. A droll improviser, he has shrewd observations on the world, albeit that his firsthand knowledge doesn’t extend beyond the garden fence (come to that, he doesn’t have any hands). Adapting their YouTube sensation to the big screen, Fleisher-Camp and Slate have come up with a singularly fresh hybrid of a movie, very much of the moment in its irreverent mix-and-match approach to different modes of storytelling.

film_slugs.title: Everything Everywhere All at Once
film_slugs.synopsis: Everything Everywhere All at Once is not only one of the bone fide hits of the year, it’s one of those movies for which the year will be remembered. The second feature from Dan Kwan and Daniel Scheinert (familiarly known as “the Daniels”), EEAAO is nothing if not upfront about its methodology. Marvel/Disney don’t own the multiverse after all: The Daniels take the concept of parallel worlds and infinite alternatives as license to reinvent what a comic book movie can be. In doing so, they subvert the fascistic architecture underpinning super hero mythology. Michelle Yeoh is Evelyn, a Chinese American whose laundromat business is being audited by the IRS. Worse, her husband Waymond (Ke Huy Quan) wants a divorce. Meanwhile she’s trying to put on a brave face for the benefit of her demanding dad (James Hong), while her rebellious daughter Joy (Stephanie Hsu) wants to make to come out of the closet with her girlfriend Becky (Tallie Medel), who’s neither Chinese nor a man. As if all this weren’t enough, she discovers that the universe – and indeed, the entire multiverse – is on the verge of implosion through the nefarious activities of one Jobu Tupaki… who just happens to be an alternate of Evelyn’s daughter, Joy. Confused? You will be… This movie has an awful lot going on, including an extended Pythonesque gag about stillness. It’s sometimes anarchic, sometimes absurd, sometimes laboured and shrill, but it throws more ideas at the screen than any action blockbuster in a very long time. There are wonderful, poignant performances from Michelle Yeoh, Ke Huy Quan and Jamie Lee Curtis (to name but three) and if we’re in luck, the comic book movie will never be the same again…

film_slugs.title: Decision to Leave
film_slugs.language: In Korean and Chinese with English subtitles
film_slugs.synopsis: Virtuoso Korean filmmaker Park Chan-wook (The Handmaiden; Oldboy) picked up rave reviews and the Best Director Prize at Cannes for this singularly strange genre piece, a neo-noir mystery about a homicide detective who falls in love with the widow (Lust Caution’s Tang Wei) of an apparent suicide. The deceased was a mid-level bureaucrat in the department of immigration, and an older man; the widow is young, beautiful, and Chinese. She’s so apologetic about her Korean she uses a translation app on her phone during her questioning, but her words don’t allay suspicion in either language, and it emerges that the dead man used to beat her. Then again, she has an ironclad alibi. These narrative elements are familiar from a 1001 thrillers. And yet we must be on our game; Park shuffles the pack with dizzying skill, concocting a visual syntax which is wildly original and quite idiosyncratic. Scenes are highly abbreviated, and often distilled into a handful of eye-catching compositions; he’ll place an unusual emphasis there or here, and it’s only later that everything falls into place. This is a tantalizing, teasing movie, and in its own weird way, desperately romantic.

film_slugs.title: Holy Spider
film_slugs.synopsis: “My intention was not to make a serial killer movie. I wanted to make a movie about a serial killer society. It is about the deep-rooted misogyny within Iranian society, which is not specifically religious or political but cultural. … Instead of making another movie about different ways a man can kill and mutilate women, we want to underline the complexity of the issue and the stakes on different sides, especially on behalf of the victims.” Ali Abbasi The strongest, most compelling, disturbing and provocative serial killer movie since David Fincher’s Zodiac, Holy Spider is an Iranian film produced in Denmark, shot in Jordan, and written and directed by the brilliant Ali Abbasi (Border). It is based on a true story. In 2001 the holy city of Mashhad, Iran, became the hunting ground for a killer nicknamed The Spider. Sex workers were his prey. This man, eventually identified as Saeed Hanaei, believed he was acting in the service of God and his city by “cleansing” the streets. Many people agreed with him. For the film, Abbasi invented the character of an intrepid female reporter dismayed by the ineptitude and complicity of the authorities, and determined to track down the killer. Actress Zar Amir Ebrahimi won the Best Actress award at Cannes for this role. Content consideration: This film contains graphic violence designed to shock and disturb.

series.description: This year’s showcase includes just two US movies ( Everything Everywhere All at Once ; Marcel the Shell With Shoes On ), two from Iran ( Hit the Road and a taboo-breaking thriller, Holy Spider , even if Ali Abbasi’s film is technically Danish); Charlotte Wells’ heartbreaking souvenir of a holiday with her father, Aftersun is a Scottish film set in Turkey; the uproarious Swedish Cannes-winner Triangle of Sadness ; Park Chan-wook’s South Korean noir romance Decision to Leave ; and Thai director Apichatpong Weerasethakul’s fantasy drama Memoria , set in Colombia. From Nunavut, we champion Nyla Innuksuk’s spirited teen girls v zombies horror flick, Slash/Back , a genre movie with a fresh Indigenous spin. And finally, on New Year’s Day, the astonishing Indian blockbuster RRR , simply the most fun we had in a cinema this year.

series.name: Best of 2023

film_slugs.title: Oppenheimer
film_slugs.synopsis: Christopher Nolan is one of contemporary cinema’s great engineers. He sets himself a formal challenge and proceeds to solve it, like a scientist testing a hypothesis. So it makes makes that he finds his most substantial subject in Robert Oppenheimer, a theoretical physicist compelled to apply theory to the very practical concern of constructing the ultimate weapon. Not one for linearity, Nolan approaches Oppenheimer’s life as if it’s a Rubik’s Cube, twisting from his student days in Europe to working on the Manhattan Project in Los Alamos back to teaching at Berkeley to his womanizing to the witch hunts of the 1950s and back, and back, a puzzle to piece together first one way and then another until everything clicks into place. This is big, ambitious, serious moviemaking, and two years after Tenet failed to persuade audiences back into cinemas, Oppenheimer did the trick. That in itself is worthy of celebration. It will amass Oscar nominations across the board, and Cillian Murphy and Robert Downey Jr are front-runners in the actor categories.

film_slugs.title: Afire
film_slugs.synopsis: Christian Petzold (Transit; Phoenix) returns with this multilayered, serio-comic portrait of a sulky writer struggling with his second novel at his friend’s summer cottage. Leon (Thomas Schubert) is stuck inside his head most of the time. So much so, he’s not really alive to what’s going on around him, except in as much as it effects him negatively. Luckily his pal Felix is the easy going sort. Leon is especially put out to find that they’re going to be sharing the cottage with a stranger, Nadja (Paula Beer), who turns out to be lovely, which is even more annoying… This is a distinct change of pace from Petzold’s recent work, albeit it’s the second in an elemental trilogy after the watery fable, Undine. The fires here are anything but metaphorical: the forests are ablaze close enough to the cottage to deter tourists and close some roads, if not to concern Leon unduly. Peevish and insecure, he is his own worst enemy — even he knows it. He uses an impending deadline to have a thoroughly miserable time, without actually accomplishing much in the way of solid work. (Who can relate?) When his publisher visits to work with him on the manuscript matters come to a head, and all the movie’s sharp nuances fall into place. Petzold’s Miroirs No.3 (also starring Paula Beer) shows in VIFF next month.

film_slugs.title: The Eight Mountains
film_slugs.synopsis: Bruno belongs to the mountains. His family are one with the landscape. When Pietro arrives from Turin – his father has purchased a cottage as a summer retreat – the boys become fast friends. For a while, Pietro’s parents even try to arrange for a better education for Bruno. But that is not his destiny. As the years pass, the boys grow up, their friendship waxes and wanes. But when Pietro’s father dies, he returns to this friendship, these mountains, to recover what remains of the past. Husband and wife team Felix van Groeningen and Charlotte Vandermeersch (who previously collaborated on Broken Circle Breakdown) have lovingly crafted a tender rural epic about male relationships, lifetime friendship, fathers and sons, and how these things intersect with geography, because where we are – where we feel we are from – has immense implications on what we make of ourselves. Although it seems perverse to shoot the film in the nearly square Academy ratio, the majesty of the alps is a vivid constant here.

film_slugs.title: Asteroid City
film_slugs.synopsis: Asteroid City is a lot. It probably needs a few viewings to unpack it. There are narrative frames within frames, it’s a film of a live 1955 TV documentary about the creation of a TV play about a bunch of teen science whizz kids convening in a crater in Nevada and being visited by a UFO… It’s shot in Wes Anderson’s patently ersatz style, a mise-en-scene that increasingly aspires to the condition of animation; quirky caricatures enacting surreal slapstick against painted backdrops. So yes, it’s a comedy, densely packed with acerbity, absurdity, parody and all those good things. But Anderson knows that comedy can also be a vehicle to explore anxiety, disconnection, mortality… Few dramas this year can match Asteroid City for its emotional reach and melancholy. That it also stands in stark formal relief from Christopher Nolan’s sober and self-important Oppenheimer should not let us overlook how Anderson’s movie distills the spiritual malaise ushered in by the Atomic Age with exacting precision.

film_slugs.title: Bottoms
film_slugs.synopsis: “A movie about empowering women (the hot ones),” as the poster puts its. Canadian Emma Seligman’s second film (after Shiva Baby) is simultaneously a raunchy high school comedy of the kind Seth Rogen used to make, and a hilarious parody of the same. Shiva star Rachel Sennott (who also cowrote the screenplay) is PJ, a loser with one friend to her name, Josie (Ayo Edibiri from The Bear). Sapphically-inclined, the pair are determined to lose their virginity, preferably to cheerleaders, and almost accidentally hatch a plan to form a fight club, for girls, to make it happen. Surprisingly, this works. But the club becomes so successful things soon spiral out of control, morality-wise. Blithely mixing deapan comic improv with quirky absurdist touches (the football team wears their uniform 24/7) and always pushing beyond the boundaries of good taste, Bottoms dares to be free and true to nothing but its own daffy urge to punch up and kick ass.

film_slugs.title: Past Lives
film_slugs.language: In English and Korean with English subtitles
film_slugs.synopsis: Two men and a woman between them sitting at a New York bar. She’s deeply engaged in conversation with one man, the other seems excluded — this would be her husband. Celine Song’s lovely feature debut Past Lives begins with this scene and then transports us to South Korea two decades earlier, and two 12-year-old classmates, Na Young and Hae Sung, negotiating an awkward first date. Soon afterwards, Na Young and her family move to the US and change their name. She becomes Nora Moon and loses touch with Hae until, in their early 20s, they’re reconnected on Facebook. And so the stage is set for a subtle, intimate romantic drama exploring deep emotional connection and the forces that pull us apart. Please note that the screenings on Jan 7-9 & 11 are not part of the Best of 2023 series and are therefore not valid for the Best of 2023 ticket pack.

film_slugs.title: Fallen Leaves
film_slugs.synopsis: Aki Kaurismäki is a genre unto himself. If you’ve seen Le Havre or The Other Side of Hope then you know what to expect, and if you haven’t, they’re charming, deadpan comedies about working class people, often melancholic or just alcoholic, but goodhearted and kind to dogs (there’s always a dog). In Fallen Leaves, Ansi (Alma Pöysti) meets Holappa (Jussi Vatanen), and these two lonely souls feel they are meant for each other, but Fate seems to have other ideas… From this simple love story, Kaurismäki crafts a poignant and pertinent comedy which picked up the Jury Prize at Cannes.

film_slugs.title: Anatomy of a Fall
film_slugs.synopsis: Justine Triet became only the third woman to win the top prize at Cannes, the Palme d’Or, for this engrossing psychological mystery thriller. In Anatomy of a Fall, when Samuel is found dead outside his alpine cabin it’s clear he plunged from the attic window. But did he jump, or was he pushed? The physical evidence is inconclusive, but the widow, Sandra looks eminently suspicious to the French authorities: she’s a prickly German novelist, and there is a recording of a violent argument she had with Samuel just the day before his death. The only other witness who might shed some light on the case is the couple’s visually impaired ten-year-old son. But as the trial proceeds, his faith in his mom’s innocence is shaken. Triet (who cowrote the screenplay with her life partner, Arthur Harari) proves there is still life in the courtroom drama. The film follows twin tracks: the nitty gritty interpretation of the evidence (which as the title suggests, hinges on the fall itself), and concurrently an anatomy of Sandra and Samuel’s marriage, which, in his absence, largely becomes an interrogation of her moral character. Layered and complex, this is compelling drama with a knockout performance from Sandra Hüller (Toni Erdmann).

film_slugs.title: BlackBerry
film_slugs.synopsis: A Canadian success story that turned into a fiasco provides the raw material for one of the standout comedies of the year… a Canadian success story, in fact. It’s a kind of anti-Social Network. Matt Johnson (The Dirties; Operation Avalanche) opts for a raw, ragged handheld style, a bit like TV’s The Office. It’s 1996, and engineer Mike Lazaridis (Jay Baruchel) and his business partner and best friend Douglas Fregin (Johnson) are on the edge of creating the world’s first Smartphone. Unfortunately their tech savvy is not matched by their business acumen, and they struggle to keep their company, Research in Motion, afloat. Everything changes when seasoned executive Jim Balsillie (a knockout performance from Glenn Howerton) signs on, bringing with him the seed money and ruthlessness needed to create and sell a prototype of their invention. Seemingly overnight the three men revolutionize the way people work, communicate and connect. They have the world at their feet, but their heads just aren’t in the game. Whip-smart, very funny and very Canadian, BlackBerry is a comedy about how capitalism inevitably eats itself. Even the winners lose.

film_slugs.title: Maestro
film_slugs.synopsis: Bradley Cooper’s second film as director is an ecstatic, symphonic portrait of the composer/conductor Leonard Bernstein, with Cooper in the title role and Carey Mulligan as his wife, the actress Felicia Montealegre. Bernstein was bisexual, but like A Star is Born, Maestro is a searing love story caught up in an imbalanced relationship; Lenny loves Felicia almost as much as he loves himself, but she refuses to play second fiddle. Cinematically daring, musically exhilarating, and with two knockout performances, this is one of the films of the year.

series.description: This was the year audiences came back to the movies… even if strikes and the lingering effects of Covid spelled a slower release rollout than many would have liked. That said, here at the VIFF Centre, we have shown over 400 different movies outside of the festival, and enjoyed a fantastic response from our community (thank you!).

series.name: Best of 2024

film_slugs.title: Challengers
film_slugs.synopsis: After an uneven patch Luca Guadagnino (Queer; Call Me By Your Name) snaps back into gear with this perversely entertaining screwball comedy masquerading as a sports film. Josh O’Connor and Mike Faist are up and coming tennis prodigies and fast friends who both have the hots for Zendaya — who’s a little ahead of them in the game (and stays that way). Volleying back and forth across a decade or so with the speed of a championship rally, Challengers charts the oscillating fortunes of this trio of frenemies on the court, and in courtship. The editing (Marco Costa) and cinematography (by Thai DP Sayombhu Mukdeeprom) in this movie are dazzling to behold, and the Trent Reznor/Atticus Ross score is simply bonkers. If Lubitsch had been born a century later, he’d undoubtedly be making sexy, fun, lightly subversive films like this.

film_slugs.title: The Monk and the Gun
film_slugs.language: In Dzongkha and English with English subtitles
film_slugs.synopsis: This delightful Bhutanese comedy keeps you guessing about just why a venerable Buddhist monk sends a disciple out into the world to bring him back a gun. As fortune and farce dictate, this mission coincides with the arrival of an American smuggler intent on clinching a deal for an antique rifle, not to mention civic rehearsals for what will be Bhutan’s first ever democratic elections (the film is set in 2006, just ten years after western media — TV and the internet — had been allowed to infiltrate this deeply conservative society). From the director of Lunana: A Yak in the Classroom, the first Bhutanese film to be nominated for an Academy Award, The Monk and the Gun is a gentle, joyful movie offering shrewd and surprising reflections from a culture far-removed from our own. Reminiscent of the freewheeling ensemble comedies of Robert Altman and the proudly parochial satires produced by Ealing Studios in post-war Britain, the film takes aim at American gun culture and western values in general. It’s beautifully measured, nicely played, and lovely to look at.

film_slugs.title: About Dry Grasses
film_slugs.synopsis: A photographer with intellectual pretensions, Samet (Deniz Celiloglu) can’t wait to serve his fourth and final year teaching elementary school in a rural backwater in eastern Turkey. Returning from a too-brief vacation, he’s embroiled in a controversy when his favourite student, Sevim (Ece Bagci), accuses him of inappropriate behaviour. Meanwhile he’s drawn to a colleague from a nearby school, Nuray (Merve Dizdar); more so, when she seems to favour his roommate, Kenan… Over the last two decades Nuri Bilge Ceylan has established himself as one of the masters of world cinema, renowned for films of Chekhovian subtlety and depth (Winter Sleep; The Wild Pear Tree; Once Upon a Time in Anatolia). His latest is a lacerating portrait of a narcissist who seemingly can’t stop himself form sowing discord and disaster. Comparisons to Lolita are justified; About Dry Grasses compels us to identify with a creep, even as it asks pointed questions about subjectivity, empathy and deep-rooted cultural divides. That Samet can also be read as a stand in for Ceylan himself (also an acclaimed photographer) gives some indication of the film’s considerable sophistication.

film_slugs.title: Good One
film_slugs.synopsis: This movie feels like a perfect short story. Seventeen-year-old Sam (Lily Collias) gamely heads out on a camping weekend with her recently remarried dad (James LeGros) and his newly divorced best friend, Matt (Danny McCarthy). Matt’s son drops out of the trip before it’s even begun, and you can’t blame him. His dad is a mess—he forgets to bring a sleeping bag—and Chris is wrapped up to tightly in his own idea of what a camping trip should be to pay attention to anybody else. The first couple of days go by in a ruefully comic register. Then on the third night, they get to telling stories around the campfire… In her debut feature, wrtier-director India Donaldson knocks this one out of the park.
